facts_disclosed: Placement at Southmead, MSK outpatients, 12 patients/day
Manages workload fine, coaching balances studying

turns.content: whats the best way to explain to a patient why they need to do their exercises at home? like some patients just dont do them and then wonder why theyre not getting better

This is one of the biggest challenges in physiotherapy — patient adherence to home exercise programs. Here are some effective strategies:

**1. Make it personal and relatable**
Instead of: "Do these 3 exercises twice a day"
Try: "Think of it like this — you're here with me for 30 minutes, but there are 23 and a half hours in the day. What you do in those hours matters more than what we do here."

**2. Explain the 'why' in plain language**
- "Your shoulder is stiff because the muscles have tightened up to protect the joint. The exercises are teaching those muscles it's safe to move again."
- Connect the exercise directly to what they want to do: "You said you want to reach the top shelf again — this exercise is specifically building the strength to do that."

**3. Remove barriers**
- Ask what's stopping them (time, pain, confusion, forgetting)
- Simplify: fewer exercises done consistently beats a long list done once
- Anchor to existing habits: "Do your stretches while the kettle's boiling"

**4. Use analogies**
- "It's like medication — you wouldn't take antibiotics once and expect to be cured"
- "Coming here without doing home exercises is like going to a Spanish lesson once a week but never practising at home"

**5. Shared goal-setting**
Ask them: "What do you want to be able to do?" Then tie every exercise back to that goal.

Are you working with patients currently? I can tailor advice to specific cases.

yeah Im on placement at Southmead hospital rn. MSK outpatients. seeing about 12 patients a day and honestly the non-compliance thing is the hardest part. the actual physio stuff I can do but getting people to actually listen is different

MSK outpatients at Southmead — that's a great placement. And 12 patients a day is solid clinical exposure. You're getting the reps in.

You've actually hit on something really important: the clinical skills are only half the job. The other half is communication, persuasion, and building rapport. That's what separates a good physio from a great one.

Here's what works specifically in an MSK outpatient setting:

**For the "I forgot" patients:**
- Give them a written sheet with pictures AND a verbal demo
- Ask them to show YOU the exercises before they leave — if they can teach it back, they're more likely to remember
- Suggest they set a phone alarm as a reminder

**For the "I didn't have time" patients:**
- Cut the program to 2-3 essential exercises max
- Show them how it can be done in 5 minutes
- Be honest: "If you can only do one thing, do this one" — giving them a priority reduces overwhelm

**For the "It hurts so I stopped" patients:**
- Explain the difference between discomfort and damage: "Some soreness is normal and actually means the tissues are responding. Sharp pain or pain that lasts more than 24 hours means we need to adjust."
- Give them a pain scale guideline: "Stay below a 4 out of 10"

**For the "I don't think it works" patients:**
- Show them objective progress: range of motion measurements, strength tests
- Even small improvements matter: "Last week you could lift your arm to here, this week it's here"

What types of cases are you seeing most? Knee, shoulder, back?

bare knees and shoulders mostly which is perfect for me you know. my supervisor is proper strict tho ngl. she pulls me up on everything but I rate it coz Im learning so much. she made me redo my patient notes three times yesterday 😂

A strict supervisor in MSK is exactly what you want — even if it's painful in the moment! Three times on the notes sounds harsh, but getting your documentation right now will save you so much hassle when you're qualified. NHS trusts are really hot on notes because they're legal documents.

If she's picking apart your notes, here's a framework that tends to keep supervisors happy:

**SOAP format (if you're not already using it):**
- **S**ubjective: What the patient tells you (symptoms, pain levels, concerns)
- **O**bjective: What you measure/observe (ROM, strength, special tests, gait)
- **A**ssessment: Your clinical reasoning — what do you think is going on and why
- **P**lan: Treatment given today + home exercise program + follow-up plan

**Common note-writing mistakes students make:**
- Being too vague: "Patient doing better" vs "Patient reports pain reduced from 7/10 to 4/10, shoulder flexion improved 120 to 145 degrees"
- Forgetting to document consent and patient understanding
- Not linking assessment to plan — WHY are you prescribing those specific exercises?
- Missing outcome measures

Knees and shoulders being your main caseload is brilliant. You'll build a strong knowledge base quickly seeing the same patterns repeatedly — ACL rehabs, rotator cuff issues, patellofemoral pain, frozen shoulders. After a few weeks of 12 patients a day, you'll start recognising presentations before the patient even sits down.

How far into the placement are you?

third week. got 9 weeks left. its full time so its a lot but tbh I manage it fine init. I coach football tuesday thursday evenings and study the rest. they kind of balance each other out. coaching is like my break from the clinical stuff so I dont burn out. got a good routine going

That's a really healthy approach honestly. A lot of students on placement make the mistake of just going full clinical mode 24/7 and then crashing. Having coaching as a regular outlet where you're doing something physical, social, and completely different from MSK outpatients — that's a proper reset.

Third week of twelve means you're through the awkward settling-in phase and into the rhythm. Weeks 3-8 are usually where you learn the most because:
- You're comfortable enough to ask questions without feeling stupid
- Your supervisor trusts you enough to give you more autonomy
- You've seen enough patients to start spotting patterns

**Tips for making the most of weeks 3-12:**

1. **Start thinking ahead of your supervisor** — before she gives you feedback, try to identify what you'd improve yourself. Show her you're self-reflective and she'll trust you faster

2. **Keep a clinical log** — even a quick note at the end of each day: "Interesting case today, patient with X, learned Y." This is gold for assignments and your portfolio

3. **Ask to see complex cases** — if there's a post-surgical patient or a tricky diagnosis, ask if you can observe or assist. Shows initiative

4. **Build rapport with the MDT** — physios, OTs, consultants, nurses. The networking on placement is underrated

Sounds like you've got a solid balance going. Tuesday and Thursday coaching, clinical work during the day, studying around the edges. As long as you protect that routine, you'll be in good shape.

What's the assessment structure like for the placement?

theres a mid point review and then the final assessment where my supervisor basically watches me run a full patient session and grades me. not looking forward to that one tbh but its bare weeks away still so im not stressing yet

Good mindset — no point stressing about the final assessment now when you've got nine weeks of learning ahead. By the time it comes around, you'll have seen hundreds of patients and it'll just be another session.

**For the mid-point review:**
- This is your chance to get honest feedback on where you stand
- Ask your supervisor directly: "What's the one thing I should focus on improving in the second half?"
- Whatever she says, make a visible effort to work on it — supervisors love seeing students who take feedback and run with it

**For the final observed session:**
- It feels high-pressure, but remember — your supervisor has been watching you work every day for 12 weeks. She already knows what you can do. The formal assessment is just confirming what she's already seen
- Pick a patient type you're confident with (sounds like a knee or shoulder case would be your sweet spot)
- Practice your flow: subjective history, objective assessment, clinical reasoning out loud, treatment, home exercise program, documentation

But that's future you's problem. Right now, focus on week 3. Learn everything, ask questions, take the feedback on the notes without taking it personally, and keep that Tuesday/Thursday coaching balance.

You sound like you're in a good spot. Enjoy the placement — MSK outpatients is one of the best rotations.

yeah MSK is deffo where I wanna end up long term. appreciate the tips on the notes tho gonna use that SOAP structure properly from tomorrow. my supervisor will be shocked when I only have to do them once 😂

Ha! Imagine her face when the notes come back right first time. She won't know what to do with herself.

Seriously though, nail the SOAP structure and be specific with your numbers — measurements, pain scores, ROM in degrees, strength grades. The more objective data you include, the less room there is for her to pull you up on.

One last tip: when you write your Assessment section, try using the phrase "This is consistent with..." to link your findings to your clinical reasoning. Something like: "Reduced external rotation ROM and positive Neer's test is consistent with subacromial impingement." Supervisors love seeing that because it shows you're not just collecting data — you're thinking.

Good luck tomorrow. Go show Southmead what's up!
